question_title: Which option will match NOAA's reported global temperature anomaly ranking for March 2026?
summary: March 2026 is very likely to rank 4th-10th warmest March on record (NOAA). January and February 2026 both ranked 5th warmest in their respective months, establishing a consistent pattern. UAH satellite data for March 2026 (+0.38°C) was unchanged from February, and Roy Spencer noted Northern Hemisphere land areas were "uneventful" despite the record-shattering US Southwest heatwave. Historical March top-3 anomalies are tightly clustered at 1.30-1.31°C (2024, 2025, 2016), far above February 2026's +1.18°C level. Copernicus preliminary data from a related forecast indicated March 2026 ranked 4th warmest. ENSO-neutral conditions (~90% probability) favor moderate but not record warmth. The background warming trend makes outside-top-10 very unlikely since all top 10 Marches are from 2015-2025.

factors.description: Jan and Feb 2026 both ranked 5th warmest in their respective months (NOAA), establishing a consistent pattern for 2026
UAH satellite lower troposphere for March 2026 was +0.38°C, essentially unchanged from Feb (+0.39°C), suggesting no dramatic global warming spike in March
Roy Spencer notes NH land areas were 'uneventful' and 'even cooler than 2024 and 2025' in March 2026 satellite data, despite US Southwest extreme heat
Top March anomalies are tightly clustered (1.31, 1.31, 1.30°C for top 3), far above Feb 2026's +1.18°C, making it hard to reach 2nd-3rd warmest
ENSO-neutral conditions (La Niña weakening, ~90% ENSO-neutral for MAM), resulting in slightly lower anomalies vs El Niño years that set records
Record-shattering US Southwest heatwave and South/SE Asian extreme heat could push global March temperature somewhat higher than Feb ranking pattern suggests
Background warming trend ensures 2026 remains very warm; all top 10 March anomalies are from 2015-2025, making outside top 10 very unlikely

reasoning:
**Forecast: March 2026 NOAA Global Temperature Anomaly Ranking**

| Option | Probability |
|--------|------------|
| (A) Warmest March on record | 3% |
| (B) 2nd-3rd warmest | 12% |
| **(C) 4th-10th warmest** | **81%** |
| (D) Outside top 10 | 4% |

**Key evidence:**
- Jan and Feb 2026 both ranked **5th warmest** in their months (NOAA), establishing a clear 2026 pattern
- UAH satellite data for March 2026 (+0.38°C) was **unchanged from February**, showing no global spike
- Northern Hemisphere land areas were **"uneventful"** in March despite the extraordinary US Southwest heatwave
- The top 3 March records (1.31, 1.31, 1.30°C) are far above Feb 2026's +1.18°C level — making warmest or 2nd-3rd unlikely
- Copernicus preliminary data (from related analysis) indicated March 2026 ranked **4th warmest**, squarely in option C
- ENSO-neutral conditions favor moderate warmth, not records
- Background warming makes outside-top-10 nearly impossible (all top 10 Marches are from 2015-2025)
sources_consulted: [(Metaculus) Which option will match NOAA's reported global temperature anomaly ranking for March 2026?](https://www.metaculus.com/questions/43062)
[Assessing the Global Temperature and Precipitation Analysis in February 2026](https://www.ncei.noaa.gov/news/global-climate-202602)
[Climate Pulse - Near real-time updates of global climate variables](https://pulse.climate.copernicus.eu)
[Hottest March Ever, Worst Fire Season Starting, Super El Niño Loading: Summer 2026 Preview](https://www.riotimesonline.com/record-heat-2026-summer-climate-crisis/)
[2026 Southwest U.S. Record Heat Wave - Climate Adaptation Center](https://www.theclimateadaptationcenter.org/2026/04/02/2026-southwest-u-s-record-heat-wave/)
resolution_criteria: This question resolves to the option that matches NOAA NCEI's official ranking for March 2026 global surface temperature anomaly on the monthly global climate report page at https://www.ncei.noaa.gov/access/monitoring/monthly-report/global/
fine_print: Use NOAA's own ranking language for March 2026. If NOAA revises the report before May 1, 2026, use the latest ranking available before that time.
